title: The tag says what it IS. The attribute says the details
steps.0.text: Add an image with `` and `src` below the sentence.
steps.1.text: Give it an `alt` that describes what can be seen in it.
checkLabels.t3: The image has an alt with text
why.empty: The editor is empty. You are adding an image, and you deleted the whole page. Bring the code back.
hint2.empty: Restore the page from the last lesson and add the image line inside the body.
why.not-closed: Some tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: `` has no closing tag. If you wrote ``, remove it.
why.no-img: There is no image. The tag is `img` — it has no closing tag, because there is nothing for it to wrap.
hint2.no-img: Write it inside `<body>`, below the sentence.
hint3.no-img: Just `` and that is all. Look at the example above.
why.no-src: The tag is there, but it does not say WHICH image. Without `src` the browser has nothing to show — you get a broken icon.
hint2.no-src: `src` says where the image is. The value is an address, in quotes.
hint3.no-src: If you have no image of your own, use `https://picsum.photos/400/250` — it returns a random one.
why.no-alt: There is no `alt`, or it is empty. A blind person reaches the image and their reader tells them… nothing.
hint2.no-alt: `alt` is the text that replaces the image. Describe what can be seen in it.
hint3.no-alt: Do not write "image". Write what is IN it: `alt="Red bicycle leaning against a wall"`.
why.useless-alt: You wrote "image" or "photo". The reader has already told the person there is an image there. You repeated it instead of saying what can be SEEN.
hint2.useless-alt: If the image disappears and your text does not replace it, your `alt` is pointless.
hint3.useless-alt: Close your eyes. Somebody is reading your page aloud. What would you need to hear to know what is there?
why.lost-content: Something disappeared from the page. You are adding an image, not deleting what you already have.
hint2.lost-content: Compare with the starting code. The heading and the sentence stay — the image is ADDED below them.
blocks.1.text: So far your tags only said what a thing IS. A heading. A paragraph. But sometimes that is not enough.
blocks.2.text: An image. The tag can say "this is an image". But WHICH image, from where? That does not fit into the tag's name.
blocks.4.marks.0.label: WHAT IT IS
blocks.4.marks.1.label: WHICH ONE
blocks.4.marks.2.label: WHAT IS SEEN
blocks.4.legend.0: Attributes go inside the opening tag, separated by spaces. Their order does not matter.
blocks.5.items.0: On the left is the name — what you are describing.
blocks.5.items.2: On the right is the value, in quotes.
blocks.6.text: You have already written two without knowing their name: `lang` on `<html>` and `charset` on `<meta>`. Same shape, same place.
blocks.8.text: By the letter of the standard the quotes are not required — a value with no spaces and no special characters passes bare. But here is what happens to a value that contains a space:
blocks.10.text: The browser sees `alt="Blue"` and then a separate attribute named `bicycle`. Half the text vanishes silently. No error, no warning — just half an `alt`.
blocks.11.text: So the rule in practice is one thing: always quotes, always double ones. Single quotes are appropriate only when the value itself contains double quotes.
blocks.13.text: There are also ones where there is nothing to specify — they are either on or off. You write the name alone:
blocks.15.text: `controls` has no value. Its presence switches on the playback buttons. These are called *boolean* attributes and you will meet more of them in the lessons on forms. You do not write `controls="true"` — that is redundant.
blocks.17.text: An attribute belongs to the opening tag. Nothing goes into the closing one — it is only a name and a slash. `</p class="note">` is a mistake, even though the browser quietly swallows it.
blocks.19.text: `src` says where the file is. Without it there is nothing to show — you get a broken icon.
blocks.20.text: `alt` is the text that replaces the image when it cannot be seen. A weak connection. A broken address. Or a blind person whose reader cannot see a picture and reads only this. It gets a whole lesson later — for now the rule is enough: describe what can be seen, do not write "image".
blocks.22.text: Add an image below the sentence. It needs two attributes: `src` and `alt`.
blocks.23.text: If you have no suitable image, use `https://picsum.photos/400/250` — it returns a random one. What matters here is the attributes, not the content.
blocks.25.items.0: An attribute is a name, an equals sign and a value in quotes.
blocks.25.items.1: It goes inside the opening tag, never the closing one.
blocks.25.items.2: A value with a space and no quotes falls apart halfway.
blocks.25.items.3: Boolean attributes have no value — only a name.
blocks.25.items.4: `src` says which image it is, `alt` says what is seen in it.
walkthrough.0.text: One line added to the body. `` has no closing tag because there is nothing to wrap — its content is the file itself.
walkthrough.1.text: The two attributes are separated by a space and their order does not matter. `alt="…" src="…"` works the same.
walkthrough.2.text: The `alt` here describes what is seen, not that something is seen. If the image fails to load, the sentence in its place still does the job — that is the test of whether it is written well.
